title: Use Causal Trend Features As A Cheap Latent Tyre-State Proxy
summary: Recent F1 tyre-degradation work models tyre pace as a latent state that evolves over laps and resets at pit stops.
rationale: Recent F1 tyre-degradation work models tyre pace as a latent state that evolves over laps and resets at pit stops. The current best solutions mostly use snapshot values plus a few lags; they do not fully exploit causal trend, acceleration, and filtered residual structure along each driver-race stint.
implementation_hint: Sort by `Year,Race,Driver,LapNumber` and create strictly causal EWM means/slopes/second differences for `LapTime_Delta`, `LapTime (s)`, `Cumulative_Degradation`, and `Position_Change`, resetting on pit stops. Add residual-to-stint-trend features, e.g. current lap delta minus EWM baseline, recent deterioration rate, and post-pit warmup trajectory over the first 1-3 laps.
expected_effect: Can capture latent cliff behavior before a pit without needing a full sequential deep model, especially in ambiguous one-stop vs two-stop zones.
risk: Trend features are easy to overfit if resets and missing chronology are mishandled; noisy races may dilute the signal.
disabled_reason: Disabled because an external duplicate-detection mechanism marked this hypothesis as duplicate of an existing hypothesis.
